coredata.dc:title: Preparation of highly sensitive Pt nanoparticles-carbon quantum dots/ionic liquid functionalized graphene oxide nanocomposites and application for H2O2 detection
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Dandan
Zhuang, Xuming
Zhai, Jian
Zheng, Yunyun
Lu, Hua
Chen, Lingxin
coredata.dc:description: There is current interest in developing carbon nanomaterials, which are a novel kind of nanomaterial, for uses in electrochemical sensing and biosensors. We constructed a novel sensor based on a Pt nanoparticles-carbon quantum dots/ionic liquid functionalized graphene oxide (PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO) nanocomposite for detecting H2O2. We characterized the morphology and electrochemical performance of the modified electrode using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and cyclic voltammetry, respectively. The unique chemical structure of PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO greatly accelerated the catalysis of H2O2 and provided plenty of active sites for electrochemical redox reactions. Electrochemical experiments demonstrated that the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO sensor had high selectivity, a wide linear range from 1 to 900μM, and a low detection limit of 0.1μM with respect to the reduction of H2O2. These characteristics indicate good electrical conductivity and high electrocatalytic activity. This simple and effective method has potential applications in chemical sensors and electrochemical catalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pt nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquid
Graphene oxide
Electrochemical sensor
H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of IL-GO, CDs, and the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite
Characterization of IL-GO, CDs/IL-GO, and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs/IL-GO (A) and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO (B) with different magnifications.
(A) FT-IR spectra of IL-GO (a), CDs (b), and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO (c); (B) Raman spectra of IL-GO (a), CDs/IL-GO (b), and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO (c).
CV curves of 1.0mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1M KCl recorded using: the bare GCE (a), IL-GO/GCE (b), CDs/IL-GO/GCE (c), and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE (d). The scan rate was 100mVs−1.
CV curves of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in 0.02M PBS (pH 7.4) with increasing concentrations of H2O2 (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10mM).
Amperometric responses of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE at −0.08V to successive additions of H2O2 in 0.02M PBS (pH 7.4). Inset: the plot of response current vs. concentration of H2O2.

Amperometric response of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE to additions of 0.1mM of H2O2 (first and last additions) and to additions of 1.0mM UA, 1.0mM AA, 0.1mM DA, and 1.0mM glucose at −0.08V constant applied potential.
Method recoveries for H2O2 determination in FBS samples (n =5).

head.ce:title: Preparation of highly sensitive Pt nanoparticles-carbon quantum dots/ionic liquid functionalized graphene oxide nanocomposites and application for H2O2 detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes and Ecological Remediation, Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Yantai 264003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Coastal Environmental Processes and Ecological Remediation
Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemical sensor based on the composites of Pt nanoparticles modified carbon dots and ionic liquid-functionalized graphene oxide was fabricated for detection of H2O2 with high sensitivity and adequate selectivity.

There is current interest in developing carbon nanomaterials, which are a novel kind of nanomaterial, for uses in electrochemical sensing and biosensors. We constructed a novel sensor based on a Pt nanoparticles-carbon quantum dots/ionic liquid functionalized graphene oxide (PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO) nanocomposite for detecting H2O2. We characterized the morphology and electrochemical performance of the modified electrode using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and cyclic voltammetry, respectively. The unique chemical structure of PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO greatly accelerated the catalysis of H2O2 and provided plenty of active sites for electrochemical redox reactions. Electrochemical experiments demonstrated that the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO sensor had high selectivity, a wide linear range from 1 to 900μM, and a low detection limit of 0.1μM with respect to the reduction of H2O2. These characteristics indicate good electrical conductivity and high electrocatalytic activity. This simple and effective method has potential applications in chemical sensors and electrochemical catalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a promising carbon material because of its large surface area, fast electron transfer [1], and exceptional thermal, chemical, and mechanical properties [2]. It is a novel kind of two-dimensional material with honeycomb crystal lattices that are based on a form like the benzene six-membered ring [3]. Graphene oxide (GO) has a single atomic layer structure that can be stretched laterally [4]. GO has a large number of polar oxygen groups, which causes both high chemical activity and hydrophilic activity between the layers of GO [5]. During the preparation of GO, the introduction of oxygen-containing groups destroys the π-conjugated structure of natural graphene, weakening electroconductivity but retaining the function of GO as an electron receptor [6–8]. Additionally, the increased hydrophilicity facilitates electrochemical modification, which enables GO to act as a carrier material for nanoparticles [9,10]. GO can be modified in situ on an electrode and, as such, can be directly used in electrochemical applications [11]. Thus, the surface modification of GO has become a focus of research. Of the various ways that GO has been functionalized for different applications, ionic liquid functionalized GO (IL-GO) has particular advantages, such as strong electrical conductivity, a large specific surface area, good dispersibility, and stability [12,13]. Because of superior solubility and surface charge, the IL-GO prepared by Niu and coworkers, showed good dispersibility and long-term stability in various solvents [14]. In addition, electrochemical sensors and biosensors based on the direct electron transfer of IL-GO with respect to various redox enzymes and their use in detecting various types of compounds, such as glucose [15], nitrite [16], and NADH [17], have been reported extensively in the literature.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are novel carbon nanostructures that are less than 10nm in dimension and were first discovered in 2004 [18] during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes using electrophoresis. Since then they have attracted a great deal of interest because of their novel, unique, and benign nature. CDs have enormous potential in luminescence, catalysis, and sensors because of their superior electronic properties as electron donors and acceptors [19]. In the past few years, CDs have had promising applications in various fields [20], such as catalysis [21], electronic [22] and photo-catalysis [23], sensors [24], bioimaging [25], optoelectronics, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering because of their optical properties. In particular, CDs have promising uses in fluorescence emissions and other exclusive characters [26]. Therefore, wide attention has been focused on the structure, establishment, and use of CDs as remarkable materials for sensors and electrocatalytic preparations.

In recent years, researchers have prepared a series of CDs that have similar structures and compositions using a variety of methods or materials. The CDs were thus modified via deposition or coating methods [27] to fabricate biosensors, for instance, combined with metal particles, metal compounds, and organic polymers. So far, there are several research groups that have prepared CDs-metal nanocomposites to improve catalytic performance because of the outstanding electrocatalytic activity that noble metals exhibit [28,29]. Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs) are an excellent electrocatalyst material and have been widely used for detecting H2O2 because of how PtNPs promote the evolution of H2O2 and reduce the H2O2 overvoltage and thus easily avoid anodic interfering substances like uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA) [30]. The superior performance of PtNPs, such as good catalytic activity, high electron transfer efficiency, and good optical properties, make them potentially applicable in catalysts, fuel cells [31], and biosensors. In recent years, decorating PtNPs on nanomaterials, such as Au and graphene, has been reported to enhance electrocatalytic activity and to provide more active sites for attaching the substrate [32]. GO can be used as Pt nanocomposite support material because of its high specific surface area, adjustable surface functional groups, good conductivity, and good compatibility with a Pt precursor solution [33]. In addition, the surface functional groups of carbon materials, especially oxygen-containing functional groups, play an important role in improving the catalytic activity of PtNPs [34]. The synergy between CDs and GO causes PtNPs to stick on the support material and to improve the stability of PtNPs.

In this study, a PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanohybrid was prepared to modify the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for the electrochemical determination of H2O2. Subsequently, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and other methods were used to investigate the structure of the obtained PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE, and cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrode. The prepared sensor has outstanding performance in the determination of H2O2 with high sensitivity, a wide linear range, and a low detection limit.

GO was purchased from Nanjing XFNano Materials Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). H2PtCl6·6H2O, ethylenediamine, 1-methylimidazole, and 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide were obtained from Sigma (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA), glucose, UA, AA, potassium ferricyanide, sodium citrate, and H2O2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were analytical grade and were used as received. Nanopure deionized and distilled water (DDW, 18.2MΩcm−1) was used in all of the experiments.

Electrochemical studies were performed on a CHI660D electrochemical system (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) equipped with a personal computer for data storage and processing. The measuring unit was a conventional three-electrode system with a GCE as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl/KCl (saturated) electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. SEM was performed on an S-4800 electron microscope (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan) at 300kV. The UV–vis absorption spectra (UV–vis) was recorded using a Thermo Scientific NanoDrop 2000/2000C spectrophotometer. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IL-GO, CDs, and the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: IL-GO was synthesized by an epoxide ring-opening reaction between GO and the 1-(3-aminopropyl)-3-methylimidazolium bromide (IL-NH2), according to the method described in our previous report [35]. 5mg of GO reacted salt effect with 10mg of IL-NH2 and 10mg of NaOH were added to the mixture and ultrasonicated for 10min. The mixture was then subjected to an oil bath reflux for 24h at 80°C. The resulting substance was centrifuged three times and dried to obtain IL-GO.

CDs were prepared according to Xu’s method [36], and the process was as follows: 25mL of sodium citrate solution (0.1M) and ethylenediamine (300μL) were loaded into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave that was kept at 180°C for 6h. The product was used after filtering with a cylinder membrane filter (0.22μm).

To prepare the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanohybrid, 10μL of CDs (1mgmL−1), 500μL of IL-GO, and 40μL of H2PtCl6·6H2O solution (17mM) were mixed and then 2.2mg of NaBH4 was added. The mixture was successively ultrasonicated for 10min and centrifuged three times.

Before modification, the GCE was polished to be mirror-like using aluminite powder, washed with DDW, and subjected to an ultrasonic bath successively in DDW and ethanol three times. The GCE was then dried at room temperature. To modify the electrode, 5μL of the obtained PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite was dropped on the cleaned GCE surface and dried in air before the next process to obtain the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE. CV at a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1 was used to characterize the different modified electrodes in a 1.0MK3Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.1M KCl. Finally, the amperometry signals were recorded at a constant potential (−0.08V).

To study the apparent characteristic and optical behavior of the CDs, TEM and UV–vis were applied in detail. TEM was performed to determine the size of the nanoparticles. Fig. S1A shows the TEM images of the CDs and confirms that the CDs were definitely sized spheres with sizes ranging from 5 to 10nm in diameter. As shown in Fig. S1B, the solution of CDs had a strong UV–vis absorption peak at 343nm, which may arise from the π-π* transition of the nanocarbon particles and from the excitation energy sink of the surface states. A maximum fluorescent emission wavelength was at 462nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of IL-GO, CDs/IL-GO, and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of the different synthesized nanomaterials were investigated using SEM. Fig. S1 shows the typical SEM images of IL-GO (A, B), CDs/IL-GO (C, D), and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO (E, F). The SEM images (Fig. S2A and B) reveal that IL-GO was a uniform sheet with microscopic crumples and wrinkles that generated a large surface area, which facilitated fixing the material and reducing the PtNPs-CDs. For comparison, typical SEM images of the CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite are presented in Fig. S2C and D. As can be seen, the distribution of CDs on the surface of IL-GO was a uniformly close film with a spherical mold, and thus, it had features of misty wrinkles. Furthermore, Fig. S2E and F show the SEM images of PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO. It is clear that the nanocomposite had a structure covered with agglomerations and distinct crumples with patchy distribution. In addition, PtNPs-CDs were discovered on the surface of the IL-GO in the shape of sheets.

The structure of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO was further characterized using TEM. Fig. 1 A and B shows the TEM result of prepared CDs/IL-GO and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO. The CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite had a nanosized structure with sizes ranging from 5 to 10nm in diameter, causing uniform dispersion and adsorption on the support. Compared to the CDs, the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanocomposite was not an ideal sphere with sizes about 8nm in diameter.

To further investigate, FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy was used to improve the characterizations of GO, PtNPs-CDs (in Fig. S3), IL-GO, CDs/IL-GO and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO (in Fig. 2 ). The prepared nanomaterials were diluted to colorless for FT-IR and Raman detection. In Fig. S3, GO has a pair obvious peaks at 3399 and 1650cm−1, which attribute to OH and CC (a). As shown in Fig. 2A, compared with GO, the peak at 2728cm−1 is owed to NH stretching vibrations of CH3(N) and CH2(N) from the imidazolium ring of IL-GO (a) [37]. The peaks at 3460cm−1 and 1639cm−1 are the typical absorption band of OH and the CC stretching vibration band in CDs (b), respectively. Meanwhile, the curve b of Fig. S3 shows no IR signals for PtNPs. In the curve c, the typical peeks of absorption band are weaker than CDs, the stretching vibration of the NH bonds in the imidazole ring is still observed at 2981cm−1, and a CN bond at 1062cm−1 indicated the successful synthesis of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO. Fig. 2B shows the Raman spectra of CDs and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO. A clear pair of peaks was at 1340cm−1 (D-band) and 1610cm−1 (G-band) for IL-GO. However, the intensities of the D-band and G-band for IL-GO were lower than those for CDs/IL-GO and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO. The signal intensity increased after the introduction of the CDs, and this can be attributed to the increased polarizability resulting from the repulsive forces between IL-GO and the CDs. The signal corresponding to the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO was distinctly reduced, and this was because the absorption of the PtNPs on IL-GO reduced the polarizability. All of these results suggest that the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanohybrids formed successfully.

To investigate the stability of PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO suspension, the pictures of prepared nanometerials were taken after stewing for various times. The result was shown in Fig. S4 which demonstrates a superior stability.

CV and amperometry were used to investigate the electrochemical behavior of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in K3Fe(CN)6 and the performance with respect to H2O2 electro-reduction and to assess its performance in electroanalytical determination, respectively.

CV was performed to investigate the electrochemical properties. The CV curves of the bare GCE, IL-GO/GCE, CDs/IL-GO/GCE, and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in a 1.0M K3Fe(CN)6 solution containing 0.1M KCl are shown in Fig. 3 . The CV curve of the bare GCE indicates reversible redox behavior attributed to electron transfer, whereas for the IL-GO/GCE, CDs/IL-GO/GCE, and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE, there are certain increases in the peak current. Compared to the bare GCE response, the increases in the current response of the other electrodes indicate that IL-graphene had an imidazole cation interacting with the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− ion via strong electrostatic interactions. When CDs and IL-GO were present at the same time, the synergy of the two increased the conductivity because of the negative charges and the electrocatalytic activity of the CDs. Additionally, on the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE, the negative charges of the PtNPs caused a higher electron transfer rate between the surface of the electrode and the solution. The data confirmed that the modified sensing interface was successfully developed.

For further investigate the respond of different electrodes toward H2O2, the CV curves of the bare GCE, IL-GO/GCE, CDs/IL-GO/GCE, and PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in 0.02M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 1mM H2O2 are shown in the Fig. S5. Apparently, there was no respond of the bare GCE to H2O2, while a tiny signal for IL-GO/GCE. The CV curve of CDs/IL-GO/GCE reveals an obvious reduction peak indicating that CDs can promote the reaction of H2O2. Compared with CDs/IL-GO/GCE, PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE has a stronger signal which demonstrates the superior catalysis performance of PtNPs.

More CV experiments were conducted to identify the H2O2 catalysis on the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE surface. CV curves (Fig. 4 ) were recorded using the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in 0.02M PBS (pH 7.4) containing H2O2 at various concentrations with a scan rate of 50mVs−1. With these conditions, there was a negligible current response observed over the range of the potential scan without H2O2. Gradually increased reduction peaks were observed about −0.40V after the injection of a controlled amount of H2O2 (0.5–10mM), and this is attributed to the reduction of H2O2. This behavior makes the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE a suitable electrode for the quantitation of H2O2.

To explore the electron transfer rate between the surface of the electrode and the solution, the electrochemical impedance spectrogram (EIS) was performed. As shown in Fig. S6, the semicircular part at higher frequencies represents the electron transfer limited process while a linear portion at lower frequencies represents the diffusion limited process. For the curve a, it can be seen that the bare GCE shows a higher electron transfer resistance which suggests a slow electron transfer rate on electrode interface. At the IL-GO/GCE (curve b), it exhibited a smaller radius at higher frequencies indicating a lower electron transfer resistance than that of the bare GCE which showed that the presence of IL-GO facilitated the electron transfer between electrode interface and H2O2. In addition, compared with IL-GO/GCE, the interfacial electron transfer resistance reduces slightly for CDs/IL-GO/GCE but obviously for PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE which indicates that the presence of PtNPs and CDs can enhance significantly the conductivity and promote the electron transfer between electrode interface and H2O2.

The electrochemical redox behavior of H2O2 with the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE in PBS at pH values varying from 4.44 to 9.37 was investigated using CV, and the results are shown Fig. S3. The results show that pH had a significant effect on the electrochemical process, which indicates that protons were involved in the reaction at the electrode. What's more, in PBS environment, the PtNPs on the electrode surface can provide binding sites for H2O2 reactions, generate precursor sites, and then provide the amperometric signal and complete the catalytic cycle according to the following equations [38]:
H2O2 +Pt(OH)2 ↔Pt(OH)2·H2O2
Pt(OH)2·H2O2 ↔Pt+2H2O+O2

Under acidic conditions,
Pt(OH)2·H2O2 +H2PO4 − ↔Pt(OH)2

As the pH value decreasing, increase of H+ blocks the formation of binding sites which due to the precursor sites undergoes protonation to give an inactive site. However, over high pH value accelerates the backing reaction progressing which lead to the decrease of H2O2 reaction efficiency. Thus, taking into account the sensitive determination for H2O2, a phosphate buffer of pH 7.4 was chosen as the optimal pH for this experiment.

To investigate the activity parameter, CV measurements were performed using 1mM H2O2 at the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE with varying scan rates in 0.02M PBS (pH 7.4), and the results are shown in Fig. S4. As the scan rate increased from 10 to 100mVs−1, the reduction peak currents increased correspondingly, and there was a linear relationship between the current and scan rate. This result indicates that the reduction of H2O2 on the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE behaved like a typical diffusion-controlled process. This was attributed to the abundance of active sites interacting with all incoming H2O2 molecules and reflects a direct dependence between the peak current and the bulk concentration, which is an important and requirement of the sensor.

To investigate further, amperometry was applied with more concentrations of H2O2, and the linear relation between the concentration and current was explored. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE sensor had a sensitive response at −0.08V to each addition, yielding steady-state signals. Fig. 5 shows a typical amperometry curve of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE with a fast amperometric response performance, which became stable within 2s with the continuous addition of H2O2 at increasing concentrations. Under the optimal conditions, the H2O2 reduction peak current (I rd) increased with concentration in the range from 1μM to 900μM. The amperometric step values were fitted using two linear regression equations: one for H2O2 content between 1 and 100μM is I rd (μA)=−0.04033x (μM)−4.77768 (R2 =0.9806), and one for H2O2 content between 100 and 900μM is I rd (μA)=−0.00215x (μM)−8.41957 (R2 =0.9852). According to this, the detection resolution limit was 0.1μM (S/N=3). To further confirm the advantages of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE for H2O2 determination, Table S1 shows the comparison of this method with other sensors and illustrates that PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE had excellent sensitivity and a wide linear range.

To assess the selectivity of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE, several compounds (such as UA, AA, DA, and glucose) that coexist with H2O2 in real biological samples were examined to assess their potential interference with the amperometric determination of 0.1mM H2O2. The results are displayed in Fig. 6 . The plot shows no signal with respect to UA (1.0mM), AA (0.1mM), DA (0.1mM), and glucose (1.0mM), but there is a well-defined H2O2 response after the addition of 0.1mM H2O2 in both the first and last steps. The excellent selectivity of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE and its resistance to interference were because of the low working potential in the amperometric determination of H2O2.

To evaluate the stability of the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE, the prepared sensor was examined to assess the electrochemical performance with CV determination of 0.1mM H2O2. As Fig. S9 shown, the prepared sensor has similar performance at different times and the intra and inter day precision with relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.9% and 2.4% (less than 5%), respectively.

The repeatability of the sensors was examined by analysis of the same concentration of H2O2 (0.1mM) using four equally prepared electrodes. The five sensors exhibited similar response (Fig. S10) and the RSD was 1.2%. These results indicated the sensors represented excellent stability and repeatability.

Determination of H2O2 in fetal bovine serum (FBS) was used to investigate the application of the designed sensor in real sample analysis. The FBS sample solution was prepared by diluted 50 times with PBS (pH 7.4) and stored in −4°C. For determinate the sample, FBS storing solution was mixed with H2O2 standard solutions with concentrations of 50μM, 250μM, and 500μM. Measurements of H2O2 concentrations were made in the recovery rate of the spiked samples and are listed in Table 1 . The data demonstrate that the PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO/GCE may be a promising system for detecting H2O2 in real samples.

In this paper, PtNPs-CDs/IL-GO nanocomposites were successfully synthesized in a simple and efficient method and applied in the construction of a non-enzymatic biosensor for H2O2 detection. The prepared sensor had great electrochemical and catalytic activity with respect to H2O2 electro-reduction because CDs effectively improved the catalytic performance of PtNPs. The obtained results indicate that the non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor exhibited excellent electrochemical features such as low detection limit, wide linear range, high stability and excellent selectivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405132), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFC1400702), and the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (XDA11020702).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide: preparation, functionalization, and electrochemical application
Graphene-supported nanoelectrocatalysts for fuel cells: synthesis, properties, and applications
One-step electrochemical fabrication of a nickel oxide nanoparticle/polyaniline nanowire/graphene oxide hybrid on a glassy carbon electrode for use as a non-enzymatic glucose biosensor
Size dependence of energy gaps in small carbon cluster: the origin of broadband luminescence

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Adsorption characteristics of nanoporous carbon-silica composites synthesized from graphite oxide by a mechanochemical intercalation method
Easy fabrication and excellent electrical conductivity of graphite oxide/poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) nanocomposites
Carbon sheets an atom thick give rise to graphene dreams
Photoluminescent C-dots@RGO probe for sensitive and selective detection of acetylcholine
A non-volatile memory device consisting of graphene oxide covalently functionalized with ionic liquid
A functional graphene oxide-ionic liquid composites-gold nanoparticle sensing platform for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of Hg2+
Glucose oxidase and graphene bionanocomposite bridged by ionic liquid unit for glucose biosensing application
Direct electrochemistry and electrocatalysis of hemoglobin in sodium alginate film on a BMIMPF6 modified carbon paste electrode
Electrochemical determination of NADH and ethanol based on ionic liquid-functionalized grapheme
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Honeycomb architecture of carbon quantum dots: a new efficient substrate to support gold for stronger SERS
Modulation and effects of surface groups on photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity of carbon dots
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Enhanced voltammetric determination of dopamine using a glassy carbon electrode modified with ionic liquid-functionalized graphene and carbon dots
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Electrochemical biosensing platforms using platinum nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes
Silver-Nafion coated cylindrical carbon fiber microelectrode for amperometric monitoring of hydrogen peroxide heterogeneous catalytic decomposition
Nonenzymatic amperometric sensing of glucose by using palladium nanoparticles supported on functional carbon nanotubes
Enhanced catalytic combustion using sub-micrometer and nano-size platinum particles
Development of an amperometric cholesterol biosensor based on graphene-Pt nanoparticle hybrid material
Electrocatalytically active graphene-platinum nanocomposites role of 2-D carbon support in PEM fuel cells
Graphene based metal and metal oxide nanocomposites: synthesis, properties and their applications
Electrochemical determination of NADH and ethanol based on ionic liquid-functionalized graphene
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots by doping nitrogen element and its application in the detection of Fe(III)
Manganese dioxide nanosheet-decorated ionic liquid-functionalizedgraphene for electrochemical theophylline biosensing
Electrochemical oxidation of hydrogen peroxide at platinum electrodes. Part IV: phosphate buffer dependence

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dandan Chen received her BS degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, in 2015. She is studying for her master degree in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. Her current research interest is the electrochemical biosensor and detection of small biomolecules.

Xuming Zhuang received his PhD in analytical chemistry from the Department of Chemistry of Shandong University, Jinan, in 2013. In the same year, he joined in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, as a lecturer. His current research interest focuses on preparation of novel electrochemical biosensors and applications to in vivo analysis.

Jian Zhai received her BS degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, in 2016. She is studying for her master degree in College of science, Northeastern University. Her current research interest is based on biosensor analysis of phage nanoparticles.
Yunyun Zheng is studying her BS degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. She has learned about electrochemistry and hopes to further study.
Hua Lu is studying for her undergraduate degree in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University. During her university, she learns something about the electrochemical analysis.

Lingxin Chen has been a professor at Yantai Institute of Coastal Zone Research, Chinese Academy of Sciences, since 2009. He obtained his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2003. During 2004–2009, he worked at Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, and Department of Applied Chemistry, Hanyang University, respectively. His research interests include the studies of novel properties of materials such as functionalized nanoparticles & functional probe molecules for developing nanoscale biochemical analysis methods and molecular imprinting-based sample pretreatment technology.
